turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Thomas has the opinion of the court this morning in case 21-1326, United States ex rel. Schutte v. SuperValu and the consolidated case.

turns.name: JUSTICE THOMAS
turns.text: These cases come to us on a writs of certiorari to the United States Court of Appeals for the Seventh Circuit. The False Claim Act imposes liability on anyone who knowingly submits a false or fraudulent claim to the federal government as well as certain government contractors. These cases focus on the acts standard and require us to determine whether a claimant can know that its claim is false if the relevant law or contract is ambiguous. Petitioners here sued respondents, a group of companies that operate retail drug pharmacies for submitting false claims to Medicare and Medicaid. Respondents countered that they could not have known that their claims were false because the relevant legal standard was ambiguous. As respondents would have it, another reasonable person might have thought that respondents' claims were true. So respondents could not have really known that their claims were false. Petitioner's theory however, is that respondents correctly interpreted the relevant standard and if that is true, petitioners argue that respondents knew that their claims were in fact false. We hold that petitioners are correct about that narrow legal question. The acts use of the term knowingly focuses on what respondents thought and believed not what another reasonable person might have thought or believed. Accordingly respondents could have known that their claims were false if they correctly interpreted the standard and believed that their claims were false. Additionally the act defines the term knowingly to encompass both deliberate ignorance and recklessness. Both of these terms also focus on what respondents thought and believed with regard to the risk that their claims were false. For these reasons and others set forth in our opinion, we vacate and remand the judgment of the Seventh Circuit. The decision of the court is unanimous.
